did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin lesion classification and malignancy detection (specifically melanoma). The study details a custom CNN architecture with 3 convolutional blocks, max-pooling, and dense layers, trained on the ISIC dataset. It reports results including sensitivity (95.3%) and specificity (93.7%) for melanoma detection, comparing them to dermatologist benchmarks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 4th International Conference on Innovative Mechanisms for Industry Applications (ICIMIA-2025). It details a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, describes the use of the ISIC dataset (specifically a subset of 2,357 images), reports specific performance metrics (sensitivity 95.3%, specificity 93.7%), and compares these results to dermatologist benchmarks. Although it includes a literature review section, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new diagnostic model, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Skin Lesion Classification and Malignancy Detection using Convolutional Neural Network
journal: Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Innovative Mechanisms for Industry Applications (ICIMIA-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin lesions and detection of melanoma using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. The model was trained on a subset of 2,357 dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 dataset, covering nine lesion types including malignant and benign cases. To address class imbalance and overfitting, the researchers employed ESRGAN-based data augmentation and focal loss during training. The final model achieved a sensitivity of 95.3% and a specificity of 93.7% for melanoma detection. These results outperformed the reported dermatologist benchmark of 87.4% sensitivity, demonstrating the potential of AI to assist in early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of malignancy detection; prevalence of 4.8% in the study subset; achieved 95.3% sensitivity.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (9 lesion classes including Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.) and binary malignancy status (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into 9 categories using a custom CNN with a softmax output layer (9 units). It also performs binary classification for malignancy detection (Melanoma vs. others), reporting sensitivity (95.3%) and specificity (93.7%) for melanoma detection. The model predicts discrete class labels rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the Skin Cancer ISIC dataset curated by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermoscopic images with clinical metadata including confirmed diagnoses, anatomical locations, and patient demographics. The study used a specific subset of 2,357 images selected to include both malignant and benign lesions.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model (70% of the subset).
use.validation: Used for fine-tuning hyperparameters and comparing performance (15% of the subset).
use.testing: Used for unbiased evaluation of the model's diagnostic accuracy (15% of the subset).
use.other: The full ISIC archive (over 23,000 images) is mentioned as a resource, but only the 2,357-image subset was used for the experiments.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the variability of training and validation accuracy and the F1-score for melanoma detection.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN model for melanoma detection and compare it against dermatologist benchmarks.
To measure the balance between precision and recall for the melanoma class.
To compare model performance before and after ESRGAN-based data augmentation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, significance tests) was reported in the paper. The study compares model performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity) against dermatologist benchmarks using descriptive statistics and percentage improvements, but does not perform statistical significance testing.

comparison_to_baselines.note: The model's sensitivity of 95.3% outperformed the reported dermatologist benchmark of 87.4%.

training_details.data_augmentation: ESRGAN-based augmentation, random rotations (±45°), horizontal/vertical flips, brightness adjustments (±20%)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels using bilinear interpolation
Histogram equalization applied to maintain color uniformity
Pixel value scaling to the range [0, 1] to enhance model stability

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance and overfitting during training.
Geometric transforms applied to enhance model generalization.

conclusions: The study demonstrates that a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) can effectively classify skin lesions and detect melanoma, achieving a sensitivity of 95.3% and specificity of 93.7%.
The proposed CNN model outperformed the reported dermatologist benchmark sensitivity of 87.4% for melanoma detection.

Addressing class imbalance and overfitting through ESRGAN-based data augmentation and focal loss significantly improved model performance, resulting in a 28.6% increase in validation accuracy and a 38.6% improvement in melanoma recall.
The baseline model suffered from severe overfitting (20.4% accuracy gap) due to class imbalance (melanoma:nv ratio=1:15), limited model capacity, and insufficient regularization.
The integration of AI-based image analysis has the potential to assist dermatologists in early skin cancer detection, improve clinical screening efficiency, and reduce diagnostic subjectivity.

Challenges such as generalizability across diverse skin types, class imbalance, and integration with clinical workflows remain, highlighting the need for expanded and diversified datasets for future research.
important_remarks: The study uses a small, curated subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset (2,357 images) rather than the full archive, which limits the generalizability of the findings to the broader population of skin lesions.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with melanoma prevalence at only 4.8% (melanoma:nv ratio of 1:15), necessitating the use of ESRGAN-based augmentation and focal loss to mitigate overfitting.

The baseline model suffered from severe overfitting (20.4% accuracy gap between training and validation) due to the class imbalance and limited model capacity, highlighting the critical need for regularization in this domain.

The ISIC dataset lacks histopathological information, which is often crucial for differentiating certain skin cancers at a microscopic level, potentially limiting the diagnostic depth of image-based models.

Images in the ISIC dataset are sourced from various institutions, introducing potential inconsistencies in image quality and labeling accuracy that could affect the reliability of the diagnostic analysis.

The paper compares the model's sensitivity (95.3%) against a reported dermatologist benchmark (87.4%) but does not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of this improvement.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (ICIMIA-2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous validation compared to top-tier journal publications.
The model was trained on a specific subset of 9 lesion types; performance on other rare or unseen lesion types not included in this subset is unknown.

No external validation on independent datasets was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the held-out test set from the curated ISIC subset, raising concerns about real-world deployment robustness across diverse skin types and demographics.
